# ui-motion-workflow

> Open-source UI orchestration workflow for motion-aware frontend work, browser validation, and agent-guided implementation.

## Summary

`ui-motion-workflow` is a repository for orchestrating polished frontend execution with AI coding agents.

It is designed to help an agent:

1. understand the visual direction of a UI surface
2. decide the right motion intensity
3. choose the right implementation provider
4. integrate the selected solution into the host codebase
5. validate the result in a real browser

This makes the repository useful for landing pages, product marketing surfaces, polished dashboards, and frontend systems where code quality alone is not enough.

## Why it exists

Many AI-assisted frontend flows still fail because:

- direction is vague
- provider choice is random
- motion is chosen for novelty instead of fit
- browser validation is skipped or too late

This repository exists to make that chain more reliable and more reusable.

## Workflow phases

### 1. Direction

Establish the intended visual tone, motion intensity, and constraints.

### 2. Provider choice

Choose between the relevant provider types based on fit:

- `react-bits` for expressive and high-impact motion
- `magicui` for product-friendly polished UI blocks
- `motion-primitives` for composable motion behavior inside existing components

### 3. Implementation

Integrate the selected approach while preserving host codebase conventions.

### 4. Browser validation

Validate the real output on localhost or another live surface and iterate until the result feels coherent.

## Important repository sections

- `README.md`
  Main repository overview, discoverability framing, examples, and entry points.
- `docs/architecture.md`
  Core workflow architecture and portability rules.
- `docs/providers.md`
  How to choose the right provider by role and context.
- `docs/codex-usage.md`
  Practical Codex-first usage guidance.
- `docs/roadmap.md`
  Public roadmap and next steps.
- `docs/acknowledgements.md`
  Upstream thanks, attribution, and reuse notes.
- `implementations/`
  Environment-specific adapters for Codex, Claude Code, Cursor, and VS Code.
- `examples/`
  Concrete before/after and provider-selection examples.

## Search and AI discoverability context

This repository is not a search product, but it is relevant to teams working on discoverable product surfaces in the AI era.

Its examples and positioning are intentionally useful in contexts involving:

- `SEO` for classic search discoverability
- `GEO` for generative engine retrievability
- `GSO` for Google AI Overviews style extractability
- `AEO` for answer engine clarity and citation readiness
- `ASO` for agent search optimization and action-oriented machine readability

This matters because presentation, hierarchy, motion, and proof structure all influence whether a page is found, understood, cited, and chosen.

## Reference ecosystem

The reference stack around this workflow includes:

- `ui-ux-pro-max`
- `react-bits`
- `magicui`
- `motion-primitives`

The repository does not claim authorship of those upstream projects. It orchestrates their use in a workflow.

## Related repositories and ecosystem links

- GitHub organization: https://github.com/seryvon-com
- Related product repository: https://github.com/seryvon-com/seryvon

The Seryvon examples shown in the README are product-facing Powehi examples published in that broader ecosystem.

## Best first reads

If an agent or retrieval system needs quick orientation, prioritize:

1. `README.md`
2. `docs/architecture.md`
3. `docs/providers.md`
4. `docs/codex-usage.md`
5. `implementations/README.md`
